_meta.authoringNotes: Classification per Bohan and Peter (1975) and Lundberg et al. EULAR/ACR 2017 criteria (PMID 29127155). Treatment per Oddis Curr Opin Rheumatol 2020 (PMID 32453037). Cancer association per Hill et al. Lancet 2001 (PMID 11197372). Antisynthetase syndrome per Mahler et al. 2014 (PMID 24685878). Mortality data from Schiopu et al. Arthritis Res Ther 2012 (PMID 22340694).

h1Title: Polymyositis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Polymyositis is a chronic idiopathic inflammatory myopathy in which CD8+ T-lymphocytes attack non-necrotic muscle fibers, producing symmetrical proximal muscle weakness that develops over weeks to months. It is rare, affecting roughly 1-10 new patients per million adults per year, with peak onset between ages 40 and 60 and a female-to-male ratio near 2:1. The classic presentation is progressive difficulty rising from a chair, climbing stairs, or lifting objects above the head, often with high creatine kinase levels (5-50 times normal) and no skin rash. Untreated disease leads to permanent muscle atrophy, dysphagia, interstitial lung disease, and a 5-year mortality near 40%. Modern immunosuppression with corticosteroids plus methotrexate or azathioprine achieves remission or major improvement in 70-80% of patients, and treat-to-target regimens have brought 10-year survival above 75% in specialist cohorts.

keyStats.prevalence: 1-10 cases per million adults per year (incidence); estimated prevalence 9-32 per 100,000 (Furst et al. 2012, US claims data)
keyStats.demographics: Female-to-male ratio approximately 2:1; higher prevalence in adults of African and Hispanic ancestry in US registries
keyStats.avgAge: Adult-onset typical between 40 and 60 years; juvenile polymyositis is exceptionally rare (juvenile dermatomyositis is the pediatric counterpart)
keyStats.globalCases: Estimated 70,000-150,000 affected adults worldwide; widely under-diagnosed in low- and middle-income countries

definition: Polymyositis (ICD-10: M33.20-M33.29) is one of the idiopathic inflammatory myopathies, a family that also includes dermatomyositis, immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy, inclusion body myositis, and overlap syndromes. The pathogenic lesion is endomysial infiltration by CD8+ cytotoxic T-cells that surround and invade non-necrotic muscle fibers expressing aberrant major histocompatibility class I antigen, leading to fiber necrosis, regeneration, and progressive functional loss. Polymyositis is now defined more narrowly than in earlier decades: many patients previously labeled polymyositis are reclassified after biopsy as immune-mediated necrotizing myopathy or as part of an antisynthetase syndrome. The 2017 EULAR/ACR classification criteria provide a weighted score combining age at onset, pattern of weakness, skin findings, laboratory tests, and biopsy features. Antisynthetase antibodies (anti-Jo-1, anti-PL-7, anti-PL-12, anti-EJ, anti-OJ) define a clinically distinct subgroup with interstitial lung disease, mechanic's hands, arthritis, and Raynaud's phenomenon. Polymyositis is managed primarily by rheumatology in collaboration with neurology, pulmonology (for lung involvement), and gastroenterology (for dysphagia), and patients require coordinated physiotherapy, occupational therapy, and speech-language input over many years.

typesClassification.description: Adult-onset symmetrical proximal muscle weakness without rash, elevated creatine kinase, endomysial CD8+ infiltrate on biopsy, and exclusion of other myopathies. Now considered uncommon as a pure entity; many cases meet criteria for overlap or necrotizing myopathy.

Defined by autoantibodies against aminoacyl-tRNA synthetases (anti-Jo-1 most common). Features non-erosive arthritis, Raynaud's phenomenon, mechanic's hands, fever, and high rates of interstitial lung disease. Pulmonary involvement determines prognosis.

Polymyositis features alongside systemic sclerosis, lupus, Sjogren syndrome, or rheumatoid arthritis. Diagnosis requires criteria for both conditions. Treatment often requires combination immunosuppression.

Inflammatory myopathy diagnosed within three years of a malignancy. More common in dermatomyositis than polymyositis (relative risk 1.4-2.0 in polymyositis versus 3-5 in dermatomyositis). Triggers extensive cancer screening at diagnosis.

Anti-HMG-CoA reductase antibody-mediated necrotizing myopathy can follow statin exposure; presents similarly to polymyositis but biopsy shows necrosis without endomysial inflammation. Increasingly recognized as a distinct entity requiring rapid steroid taper plus IVIG.

primarySymptoms: Symmetrical proximal muscle weakness developing over weeks to months, affecting hips and shoulders before distal muscles — difficulty climbing stairs, getting out of a chair, or lifting heavy objects.
Difficulty raising the arms to brush hair, reach overhead shelves, or hold objects at arm's length.
Falls or near-falls when getting out of low chairs or off the toilet.
Neck flexor weakness causing inability to lift the head off a pillow when lying flat.
Dysphagia for solids and then liquids in 30-50% of patients, with pharyngeal pooling and nasal regurgitation.
Myalgia (mild aching of large muscles) in 25-50% — pain is usually less prominent than weakness.
Fatigue that is out of proportion to recent activity and not relieved by rest.
Unintentional weight loss reflecting catabolism and dysphagia.
Low-grade fever, especially in antisynthetase syndrome.
Dyspnea on exertion, dry cough, and crackles signaling interstitial lung disease (present in 20-40% over the disease course).
earlyWarningSigns: New difficulty rising from a chair without using arms — the Gowers' maneuver in adults
Falling when going up curbs or kerbs because the quadriceps no longer support the body
Asymptomatic creatine kinase elevation (typically 1,000-10,000 U/L) found on routine bloodwork
Mechanic's hands: fissured, scaly skin on the radial side of the fingers, in antisynthetase syndrome
Raynaud's phenomenon and non-erosive arthralgia preceding muscle symptoms by months in overlap or antisynthetase syndromes
emergencySigns: Acute aspiration pneumonia with fever, oxygen desaturation, and cough after meals — pharyngeal weakness needs urgent evaluation
Rapidly worsening dyspnea or hypoxia suggesting acute interstitial pneumonitis (high mortality, especially in anti-MDA5-positive overlap cases)
Severe respiratory muscle weakness with reduced cough and rising carbon dioxide — may require non-invasive or invasive ventilation
Rhabdomyolysis with creatine kinase above 50,000 U/L, dark urine, and rising creatinine — risk of acute kidney injury
New cardiac symptoms (chest pain, arrhythmia, syncope) suggesting myocarditis

causes.cause: Autoreactive CD8+ T-cell attack on muscle fibers
causes.description: The proximate mechanism: endomysial CD8+ T-lymphocytes recognize aberrantly expressed MHC class I on muscle fiber surfaces, releasing perforin and granzymes that cause fiber necrosis. Biopsy shows characteristic surrounding and invasion of non-necrotic fibers.
HLA-DRB1*03:01 and the ancestral 8.1 haplotype confer roughly 3-5 fold risk in European populations. HLA associations vary by autoantibody subtype, particularly anti-Jo-1 with DRB1*03:01.

Ultraviolet light exposure (geographic gradient), viral infection (Coxsackie B, HIV, hepatitis C, HTLV-1), and seasonal pollen exposure correlate with disease onset in epidemiologic studies. No single trigger has been confirmed as causative.

Statins, fibrates, anti-tumor necrosis factor agents, hydroxyurea, and immune checkpoint inhibitors (pembrolizumab, nivolumab) can precipitate inflammatory myopathy. Checkpoint-inhibitor myositis has emerged as a distinct entity with frequent myocarditis and high mortality.

Antisynthetase antibodies (anti-Jo-1, anti-PL-7, anti-PL-12, anti-EJ, anti-OJ, anti-KS), anti-SRP, and anti-Mi-2 each define clinical subsets with characteristic organ involvement, response to therapy, and prognosis.

riskFactors.description: Peak incidence in the fifth and sixth decades. Onset before age 18 is rare and usually represents juvenile dermatomyositis rather than polymyositis.
Female-to-male ratio approximately 2:1 in adult cohorts; the gap narrows in cancer-associated and overlap forms.
Adjusted prevalence is 1.5-2 times higher than in non-Hispanic white populations, partly reflecting HLA distribution. Disease severity and renal involvement also tend to be higher.
Strongest genetic association in European populations; risk ratio 3-5. Influences autoantibody profile and likelihood of interstitial lung disease.
Statins are linked to anti-HMG-CoA reductase necrotizing myopathy; checkpoint inhibitors precipitate severe myositis in roughly 1% of treated patients with notable myocarditis risk.
Coxsackie B, HIV, hepatitis C, and HTLV-1 are associated with secondary inflammatory myopathy. Pandemic-era SARS-CoV-2 has been reported as a trigger in case series.
Patients with systemic sclerosis, lupus, or Sjogren syndrome have elevated polymyositis risk through shared pathways and treatments.

affectedDemographics: Incidence highest in adults aged 40-60; cases under age 18 are uncommon and typically reclassified as juvenile dermatomyositis
US Medicare and commercial claims show age-adjusted prevalence around 14 per 100,000 for inflammatory myopathies overall
African American adults have 1.5-2 times higher age-adjusted incidence in US registries
Antisynthetase syndrome accounts for 20-30% of inflammatory myopathies in tertiary cohorts
Cancer association strongest in the 3 years before and after diagnosis; ovarian, lung, gastrointestinal, and lymphoid malignancies overrepresented
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis combines clinical history, examination, muscle enzyme testing, electromyography, autoantibody panels, imaging, and muscle biopsy. The patient's history identifies the timeline (weeks to months) and distribution (proximal greater than distal, symmetrical) of weakness. Examination distinguishes true weakness from pain-limited effort and rates each muscle group on the Medical Research Council scale. Initial laboratory workup measures creatine kinase, aldolase, lactate dehydrogenase, and aminotransferases — elevations are common but not specific. A myositis-specific antibody panel (anti-Jo-1, anti-PL-7, anti-PL-12, anti-EJ, anti-OJ, anti-KS, anti-SRP, anti-HMG-CoA reductase, anti-Mi-2, anti-TIF1-gamma, anti-NXP-2, anti-MDA5) is increasingly performed at presentation because antibody profile guides prognosis and screening. Electromyography shows the classic triad of small polyphasic motor unit potentials, fibrillations, and positive sharp waves. MRI of thighs identifies edema and active inflammation, helps target biopsy, and tracks treatment response. Muscle biopsy remains the gold standard for distinguishing polymyositis from necrotizing myopathy, inclusion body myositis, and dystrophy; needle or open biopsy is performed on an MRI-positive site avoiding recent EMG punctures. Once polymyositis is confirmed, age-appropriate cancer screening (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, mammography, colonoscopy, pelvic ultrasound, prostate-specific antigen) is offered. High-resolution chest CT and pulmonary function tests assess for interstitial lung disease.

diagnosticTests.test: Creatine kinase, aldolase, AST, ALT, LDH
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects muscle enzyme leak; creatine kinase typically 5-50 times normal in active disease
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Results return same day. Mildly elevated AST and ALT may be misinterpreted as liver disease — a gamma-glutamyl transferase or muscle enzyme panel clarifies.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Defines clinical subset (antisynthetase, necrotizing, overlap) and guides screening for lung and cancer involvement
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood draw and laboratory immunoblot or ELISA. Results return in 1-2 weeks. Anti-Jo-1 is the most common antibody (15-20% of cases).

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies myopathic motor unit potentials with spontaneous activity supporting inflammatory rather than neurogenic disease
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Outpatient procedure of 30-60 minutes. A neurologist inserts thin needle electrodes into selected muscles. Mild discomfort during insertion; minor bruising afterwards.

diagnosticTests.test: MRI of thighs or symptomatic muscles
diagnosticTests.purpose: Demonstrates active myositis (edema on STIR or T2 fat-saturation), guides biopsy, and quantifies fatty replacement in chronic disease
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: 30-45 minutes of lying still in the scanner. No contrast required for screening; gadolinium is optional for fascial assessment.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Gold-standard confirmation, distinguishes polymyositis from necrotizing myopathy, dystrophy, and inclusion body myositis
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Outpatient procedure under local anesthesia. A small skin incision and removal of a 1 cm muscle sample. Histology reports return within 1-3 weeks.

diagnosticTests.test: High-resolution chest CT and pulmonary function tests
diagnosticTests.purpose: Screens for interstitial lung disease, the leading cause of mortality in antisynthetase syndrome
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: CT takes 10 minutes. Spirometry, lung volumes, and diffusing capacity (DLCO) take 30-45 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies occult malignancy at diagnosis and during the first 3 years of follow-up
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis, mammography, colonoscopy, pelvic ultrasound, PSA, and serum tumor markers selected by patient demographics and antibody profile.

treatmentOverview: Treatment goals are normalizing strength, lowering creatine kinase, preventing extra-muscular damage, and minimizing glucocorticoid exposure. First-line induction therapy is oral prednisone 1 mg/kg daily (maximum 80 mg) for 4-8 weeks, then tapered over 9-12 months. A steroid-sparing immunosuppressant is started at the same time because monotherapy with prednisone produces high relapse rates and unacceptable toxicity. Methotrexate 15-25 mg weekly and azathioprine 2-3 mg/kg daily are the two best-supported first-line conventional agents and produce remission or major improvement in 60-80% of patients within 6 months. Mycophenolate mofetil 2-3 g daily is preferred when interstitial lung disease is present. Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) at 2 g/kg over 2-5 days every 4 weeks is used for refractory disease, dysphagia, and the recently approved EMPYRE indication for adult polymyositis after 2022 phase III trial results. Rituximab 1 g intravenously on days 0 and 14, repeated at 6 months, is reserved for treatment-resistant disease and antisynthetase syndrome with progressive lung involvement, with response in 70-80% of refractory cases. Calcineurin inhibitors (tacrolimus, cyclosporine) are options for severe lung involvement. Treat-to-target follow-up uses the Total Improvement Score and Myositis Damage Index every 3-6 months. Glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis is prevented with calcium, vitamin D, and a bisphosphonate when cumulative steroid dose exceeds 7.5 mg prednisone daily for over 3 months. Physiotherapy is initiated within 2 weeks of starting treatment to preserve range of motion and slowly rebuild strength as inflammation falls.

medicalTreatments.name: Prednisone 1 mg/kg daily (maximum 80 mg) with 9-12 month taper
medicalTreatments.description: First-line induction therapy. Initial dose is maintained for 4-8 weeks until creatine kinase normalizes and strength improves, then tapered by 5-10 mg every 2-4 weeks. Calcium, vitamin D, and bone-protective therapy are standard.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Initial clinical and biochemical response in 80-90% of patients within 8 weeks; relapse on taper in 40-60% without steroid-sparing therapy

medicalTreatments.name: Methotrexate 15-25 mg weekly (oral or subcutaneous) with folic acid 5 mg weekly
medicalTreatments.description: First-line steroid-sparing agent. Started concurrently with prednisone. Avoid in interstitial lung disease (risk of methotrexate pneumonitis). Monitor full blood count and liver function monthly.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Remission or major response in 60-70% of patients; 30-40% reduction in cumulative prednisone exposure over 2 years

medicalTreatments.name: Azathioprine 2-3 mg/kg daily
medicalTreatments.description: Alternative first-line steroid-sparing agent. Test thiopurine S-methyltransferase activity before starting to identify rapid metabolizers and patients at risk of severe myelosuppression. Monitor full blood count and liver function.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Remission rates 60-75%; superior to placebo in trials and equivalent to methotrexate in most comparisons

medicalTreatments.name: Mycophenolate mofetil 2-3 g daily
medicalTreatments.description: Preferred when interstitial lung disease is present, or when methotrexate and azathioprine are contraindicated. Stabilizes lung function in 70% of antisynthetase patients.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 60-70% improvement or stabilization of muscle strength and lung function over 12 months in observational cohorts

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous immunoglobulin (IVIG) 2 g/kg over 2-5 days, every 4 weeks
medicalTreatments.description: FDA- and EMA-approved for adult dermatomyositis (also used in polymyositis) following ProDERM phase III trial. Particularly useful in refractory disease, dysphagia, and pregnancy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Total Improvement Score response in 79% of treated patients versus 44% with placebo (ProDERM 2022); benefit sustained at 40 weeks

medicalTreatments.name: Rituximab 1 g IV on days 0 and 14, repeated at 6 months
medicalTreatments.description: B-cell-depleting therapy used in refractory disease and antisynthetase syndrome with progressive lung disease. Onset of effect at 3-6 months.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical response in 70-80% of refractory cases (RIM trial and subsequent observational studies)

medicalTreatments.name: Tacrolimus 0.075 mg/kg twice daily or cyclosporine 3-5 mg/kg daily
medicalTreatments.description: Calcineurin inhibitors used principally for antisynthetase syndrome with rapidly progressive interstitial lung disease. Trough levels and renal function require regular monitoring.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Lung function stabilization in 60-75% of patients when combined with steroids and mycophenolate

alternativeTreatments.name: Structured exercise therapy
alternativeTreatments.description: Supervised aerobic and progressive resistance training improves muscle strength and aerobic capacity without raising creatine kinase. Recommended once induction reduces active inflammation; randomized trials show 15-25% gains in maximal voluntary contraction at 6 months.

alternativeTreatments.name: Vitamin D and creatine supplementation
alternativeTreatments.description: Vitamin D 800-2000 IU daily is recommended for steroid-induced bone protection. Creatine monohydrate 3-5 g daily has small but consistent benefits on strength in randomized trials of inflammatory myopathy.

whySeeSpecialist: Polymyositis is uncommon and frequently misdiagnosed; specialist rheumatology input is essential to confirm diagnosis, select autoantibody-appropriate therapy, and avoid the common pitfall of treating fibromyalgia or polymyalgia rheumatica with high-dose steroids. Pulmonology involvement is mandatory when interstitial lung disease is present, and gastroenterology and speech-language pathology when dysphagia is identified.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a rheumatologist with myositis-clinic experience, access to a full myositis-specific autoantibody panel, and a multidisciplinary network including a neuromuscular pathologist, pulmonologist, and physical and occupational therapists trained in inflammatory myopathy. Tertiary myositis units publish outcome data and participate in trials.
hospitalCriteria: Multidisciplinary myositis clinic (rheumatology, neurology, pulmonology, dermatology)
Neuromuscular pathology with experience interpreting muscle biopsies
Access to myositis-specific and -associated autoantibody panels
Pulmonary function testing and high-resolution chest CT capability
Physical and occupational therapy services with myositis-specific protocols
keyFacilities: Bone health and metabolic clinics for steroid-treated patients
Clinical trial centers participating in myositis studies
preventionStrategies: Disclose statin and checkpoint-inhibitor exposure to clinicians evaluating new muscle weakness; early withdrawal of the offending drug shortens recovery.
Attend age-appropriate cancer screening at diagnosis and annually for the first 3 years.
Vaccinate against influenza, pneumococcus, herpes zoster, and SARS-CoV-2 before starting immunosuppression where possible.
Maintain physical activity within tolerance during remission to slow muscle deconditioning.
Adhere to prescribed taper schedules; abrupt steroid discontinuation is the most common cause of early relapse.
lifestyleModifications: Take prednisone with food in the morning to align with circadian cortisol and minimize insomnia.
Begin or continue supervised exercise within 2 weeks of starting treatment, including aerobic and resistance training tailored to current strength.
Use modified utensils, raised toilet seats, and grab bars during initial weakness; many adaptations can be discontinued as strength returns.
Sit upright for at least 30 minutes after meals if dysphagia is present, and use thickened liquids if recommended by speech-language pathology.
Monitor blood glucose, blood pressure, and weight during steroid therapy; both diabetes and hypertension can emerge within weeks.
Use sunscreen (especially in antisynthetase syndrome and overlap disease) to limit photo-aggravated skin changes.

recommended: Protein intake of 1.2-1.6 g/kg/day to support muscle rebuilding during recovery
Calcium-rich foods (dairy, leafy greens, fortified plant milks) and vitamin D to offset steroid-induced bone loss
Mediterranean-style diet emphasizing oily fish, vegetables, legumes, and olive oil
Modified textures (mechanical soft, pureed, thickened liquids) where dysphagia is present, guided by a speech-language pathologist
avoid: Excess sodium (over 2.3 g/day) while on prednisone — accelerates hypertension and fluid retention
Refined carbohydrate-heavy meals — accelerate steroid-induced glucose intolerance
Alcohol while on methotrexate, azathioprine, or active liver enzyme elevation
Grapefruit juice while on tacrolimus or cyclosporine — raises drug levels unpredictably

exerciseGuidelines: Begin gentle aerobic activity (stationary cycling, walking, water-based exercise) within 2 weeks of treatment, progressing to supervised resistance training as inflammation falls. Trials show resistance training does not raise creatine kinase or worsen disease and produces 15-25% strength gains over 6 months. Avoid exercise to exhaustion during active flares.
dailyManagement: Take medications at consistent times to maintain steady drug levels; methotrexate is taken once weekly on the same day.
Record daily activities that are difficult (stairs, rising from chair, lifting) — a useful early indicator of relapse.
Perform a 30-second sit-to-stand test once a month at home to track strength.
Inspect skin daily during antisynthetase syndrome for new rash, mechanic's hands changes, or ulceration.
Maintain hydration to support kidney function on immunosuppression.
Attend follow-up rheumatology, pulmonology, and physiotherapy appointments on the schedule agreed at diagnosis.
prognosis: With current immunosuppression, 5-year survival in adult polymyositis is 75-90% and 10-year survival around 70-80%, a substantial improvement over the 50-60% 5-year survival of the 1970s. Prognosis is dominated by extra-muscular features: interstitial lung disease (especially when anti-MDA5 or anti-Jo-1 positive), associated malignancy, cardiac involvement, and severe dysphagia carry the greatest risk. About 20% of patients achieve sustained drug-free remission, 50-60% require maintenance immunosuppression with intermittent flares, and 15-20% have refractory disease requiring rituximab, IVIG, or trial therapies. Functional outcomes are favorable when treatment starts within 6 months of symptom onset; delay beyond 12 months correlates with permanent muscle atrophy and dependence. Cancer-associated myositis has the poorest survival, dominated by the underlying tumor; antisynthetase syndrome with rapidly progressive lung disease and anti-MDA5 dermatomyositis variants have 6-month mortality up to 50%.
recoveryTimeline: Creatine kinase falls within 2-6 weeks of starting prednisone; objective strength gains lag by 4-12 weeks because muscle fiber regeneration takes longer than enzyme leak resolution. Most patients return to near-baseline function over 6-12 months. Steroid taper extends over 9-18 months. Annual flares occur in 30-40% of patients and typically respond to brief steroid pulses.
complications: Interstitial lung disease — develops in 20-40% over the disease course and is the leading cause of death; baseline and serial high-resolution CT plus pulmonary function tests are essential.
Dysphagia and aspiration pneumonia from pharyngeal weakness — requires speech-language assessment and may need percutaneous gastrostomy in severe cases.
Cardiac involvement including myocarditis, arrhythmias, and conduction disease — baseline ECG and echocardiogram at diagnosis; troponin monitoring when symptoms develop.
Cancer association — polymyositis carries a 1.4-2 fold increased risk of malignancy in the first 3 years; full age-appropriate screening at diagnosis and on relapse.
Steroid-induced osteoporosis and avascular necrosis — bone protection from day one, with bone-density scan within 3 months of starting prednisone.
Calcinosis cutis in juvenile and overlap forms — painful calcium deposits in skin and subcutaneous tissue; difficult to treat.

supportResources.name: The Myositis Association
supportResources.description: Global patient organization providing education, support groups, registry data, and clinician referrals.

supportResources.name: Cure JM Foundation
supportResources.description: Focused on juvenile myositis but provides relevant resources for adult patients and clinicians.

supportResources.name: American College of Rheumatology — Myositis
supportResources.description: Patient education on inflammatory myopathy from the leading US rheumatology society.

supportResources.name: Myositis UK
supportResources.description: UK-based patient charity offering helpline, local support groups, and research updates.

confusedWithConditions.name: Fibromyalgia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both cause muscle pain and fatigue, but fibromyalgia produces normal creatine kinase, normal electromyography, and no objective weakness; tender points dominate. Polymyositis produces true objective weakness on examination, raised creatine kinase, and characteristic biopsy findings. Steroids do not help fibromyalgia.

confusedWithConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Rheumatoid arthritis causes joint synovitis with morning stiffness and erosions; polymyositis affects muscle, not joint, although overlap syndromes occur. Anti-CCP and rheumatoid factor distinguish RA, while myositis-specific autoantibodies and biopsy findings distinguish polymyositis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Lupus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Both can cause myalgia, weakness, and arthritis. Lupus features photosensitive rash, oral ulcers, serositis, renal involvement, and ANA with anti-dsDNA or anti-Sm antibodies. Polymyositis lacks systemic features unless overlap is present; muscle biopsy shows CD8+ infiltrate in polymyositis but rarely in pure lupus myositis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Sjogren Syndrome
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Sjogren syndrome causes dry eyes, dry mouth, and parotid enlargement with anti-Ro/SSA and anti-La/SSB antibodies. Polymyositis can coexist as overlap myositis; isolated Sjogren has normal creatine kinase and no proximal weakness.

relatedConditions.name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are systemic autoimmune diseases managed by rheumatology with overlapping treatment strategies and the risk of overlap myositis.

relatedConditions.name: Lupus
relatedConditions.relevance: Common overlap with polymyositis; shared immunosuppression and similar autoantibody screening.

relatedConditions.name: Sjogren Syndrome
relatedConditions.relevance: Frequent overlap and shared autoimmune mechanisms; both treated by rheumatology.

relatedConditions.name: Fibromyalgia
relatedConditions.relevance: Important differential diagnosis for chronic muscle pain and fatigue; distinguishing the two prevents inappropriate immunosuppression.

faqs.question: What is polymyositis in simple terms?
faqs.answer: Polymyositis is a rare autoimmune disease in which the immune system attacks muscle fibers and causes symmetrical weakness in the hips, shoulders, and neck. It develops over weeks to months and is treated with steroids combined with steroid-sparing immunosuppressants.

faqs.question: What are the first signs of polymyositis?
faqs.answer: The earliest sign is symmetrical proximal muscle weakness, usually difficulty rising from a chair, climbing stairs, or lifting objects overhead. Creatine kinase levels are often raised on routine blood tests. Skin rash, joint pain, and Raynaud's phenomenon may precede weakness in antisynthetase syndrome.

faqs.question: What is the difference between polymyositis and dermatomyositis?
faqs.answer: Both cause symmetrical proximal weakness with raised creatine kinase. Dermatomyositis additionally produces characteristic skin changes including a heliotrope rash, Gottron's papules, and the shawl sign, and is more strongly linked to cancer. Polymyositis has no skin involvement.

faqs.question: How is polymyositis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis combines muscle enzyme testing (raised creatine kinase), electromyography, MRI of muscles, muscle biopsy showing CD8+ T-cell infiltration, and myositis-specific autoantibody panels. Cancer screening is also performed because of the association between inflammatory myopathy and malignancy.

faqs.question: How is polymyositis treated?
faqs.answer: First-line treatment is oral prednisone 1 mg/kg per day combined with a steroid-sparing immunosuppressant such as methotrexate or azathioprine. Mycophenolate mofetil, IVIG, and rituximab are added for refractory disease or lung involvement. Physiotherapy is started within 2 weeks of starting steroids.

faqs.question: Is polymyositis curable?
faqs.answer: Polymyositis is rarely cured outright but is controllable in most patients. About 20% achieve sustained drug-free remission, 50-60% require long-term maintenance immunosuppression with intermittent flares, and 15-20% have refractory disease needing advanced therapies such as rituximab or IVIG.

faqs.question: What is the life expectancy for polymyositis?
faqs.answer: With modern treatment, 5-year survival is 75-90% and 10-year survival is 70-80% in specialist cohorts. The main causes of reduced life expectancy are interstitial lung disease, associated cancer, infections from immunosuppression, and cardiac involvement.

faqs.question: Does polymyositis cause pain?
faqs.answer: Mild muscle aching occurs in 25-50% of patients, but pain is usually less prominent than weakness. Severe pain raises the suspicion of necrotizing myopathy, rhabdomyolysis, or an alternative diagnosis such as polymyalgia rheumatica or fibromyalgia.

faqs.question: Is polymyositis hereditary?
faqs.answer: Polymyositis is not inherited in a Mendelian pattern but susceptibility is influenced by HLA-DRB1*03:01 and other immune-related genes. First-degree relatives have a small absolute increase in risk for autoimmune disease in general but rarely develop polymyositis themselves.

faqs.question: Can polymyositis affect the lungs?
faqs.answer: Yes. Interstitial lung disease develops in 20-40% of patients over the disease course, particularly in those with anti-Jo-1 or other antisynthetase antibodies. It is the leading cause of mortality. Screening with high-resolution chest CT and pulmonary function tests is performed at diagnosis.

faqs.question: Can polymyositis affect swallowing?
faqs.answer: Dysphagia affects 30-50% of patients because the upper-pharyngeal muscles are striated and susceptible. Patients describe food sticking in the throat, nasal regurgitation, and aspiration during meals. Speech and swallowing therapy and modified diets are part of standard management.

faqs.question: Is polymyositis linked to cancer?
faqs.answer: Polymyositis carries a 1.4-2 fold increased risk of malignancy within 3 years of diagnosis. Dermatomyositis carries a higher risk (3-5 fold). Age-appropriate cancer screening is performed at diagnosis and during the first 3 years; certain autoantibodies such as anti-TIF1-gamma identify higher-risk patients.

faqs.question: What is antisynthetase syndrome?
faqs.answer: Antisynthetase syndrome is defined by autoantibodies against aminoacyl-tRNA synthetases (anti-Jo-1 most common) and features inflammatory myopathy, interstitial lung disease, non-erosive arthritis, mechanic's hands, Raynaud's phenomenon, and fever. Lung involvement determines prognosis.

faqs.question: Can statins cause polymyositis?
faqs.answer: Statins can cause anti-HMG-CoA reductase necrotizing myopathy, which resembles polymyositis with raised creatine kinase and weakness but shows necrosis rather than inflammation on biopsy. Symptoms persist after stopping the statin and usually require steroids plus IVIG.

faqs.question: Does exercise help or worsen polymyositis?
faqs.answer: Supervised aerobic and resistance exercise improves strength and aerobic capacity without raising creatine kinase or worsening inflammation. Trials show 15-25% gains in maximal voluntary contraction over 6 months. Exercise is started within 2 weeks of beginning immunosuppression.

faqs.question: What blood tests track polymyositis activity?
faqs.answer: Creatine kinase, aldolase, AST, ALT, and LDH track muscle enzyme activity. Improvement typically appears within 2-6 weeks of starting prednisone. Inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR) and KL-6 follow lung involvement. Antibody titres are not reliable activity markers.

faqs.question: Can polymyositis come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes. Relapse occurs in 30-40% of patients annually, usually during steroid taper or after dose reduction of the steroid-sparing agent. Relapses typically respond to brief steroid pulses and increased immunosuppression. Long-term remission is more likely with combination therapy from the outset.

faqs.question: Is polymyositis common in children?
faqs.answer: True polymyositis is rare in children. Most pediatric inflammatory myopathies are juvenile dermatomyositis, which has distinctive skin features and a different treatment approach. Reclassification of historical pediatric polymyositis as juvenile dermatomyositis or other myopathies is common.

faqs.question: How long do steroids last in polymyositis treatment?
faqs.answer: Prednisone is started at 1 mg/kg daily and maintained for 4-8 weeks, then tapered over 9-12 months. Many patients remain on a low maintenance dose (5-10 mg daily) for 1-2 years. Steroid-sparing immunosuppressants are added to limit cumulative exposure and side effects.

faqs.question: Is IVIG effective in polymyositis?
faqs.answer: Yes. The 2022 ProDERM trial showed Total Improvement Score response in 79% of patients treated with intravenous immunoglobulin versus 44% with placebo at 16 weeks. IVIG is now FDA- and EMA-approved for adult dermatomyositis and used in refractory polymyositis and dysphagia.

faqs.question: Can polymyositis affect the heart?
faqs.answer: Cardiac involvement occurs in 10-30% of patients and includes myocarditis, conduction disease, arrhythmia, and rarely cardiomyopathy. Baseline ECG and echocardiogram are standard at diagnosis. Troponin monitoring is added when new chest pain, dyspnea, or palpitations develop.

symptomKeywords: cannot rise from chair without arms
metaDescription: Polymyositis is an autoimmune muscle disease causing proximal weakness. Symptoms, autoantibodies, steroids, and prognosis, medically reviewed.
